I wrote my college thesis and throughout part of the process was submitting progress to Turnitin. While the primary tool in the platform is plagiarism detection, AI detection was added later and it terrified everyone. However, we were told we were allowed to use AI, to quote it correctly and cite it. We were told we were to fact check it, to use it as an editing tool and to use it for sources. I don't think AI checking shouldn't be the bar with which to measure the quality of a work, because there are academically correct ways to use it. In the end, the final say is not in the hands of the systems themselves, but the people that trust the systems. And that's the biggest issue. Humans, being educators and academic authorities, need to form a critical criteria to determine the validity of a work, and need to put in place methods that will evaluate the real final purpose of the works being given: for students to actually retain certain information and demonstrate their capacity to do research. The act of redaction in itself is important, yes, but the objective is ultimately to give students the tools to think critically and with information. It really is a fine line, because some will indeed use these tools as devices with which to make the effort disappear completely, instead of using them to ease the load on the student. Again, this would be solved by evaluating the student's knowledge on the subject after the work has been done. We have been copy-pasting without reading forever, and how that hasn't been solved yet is beyond me.

My takeaways for your ease >>> >> Core Skills for Personal Growth in future Be curious about how things work and explore creative ways to solve problems. Think in systems, understand how different parts interact. Understand interactions and be able to explain systems clearly. Develop strong observation and curiosity. Learn to teach yourself difficult things. >> Guidance for Parents Encourage learning from first principles. Teach discipline and create healthy friction in the learning process. Emphasize the long-term benefits of hard work and getting thinks done. Help children build the mindset of finish what they start - you don't always have to feel “happy” at every moment. >> For students (especially those aged ~17–26), the focus should be on identifying real problems you enjoy, envisioning where you want to be in 10 years, then working backwards to build the skills and portfolio that get you there. STEP 1 – Pick a 10-Year Direction Ask yourself: What problems do I genuinely enjoy thinking about? What job do I secretly want in 10 years? What does an ideal workday look like for me? This becomes your initial draft direction. STEP 2 – Reverse Engineer Success Study the people already where you want to be. Understand their path. STEP 3 – Choose the Fastest Path to Skills Bootcamps, focused programs, self-study, and online learning. Always ask: What gets me the skills and portfolio the fastest within my budget? STEP 4 – Build Proof of Work Not just a CV. Create projects, code, designs, case studies, content - anything that shows what you can actually do. STEP 5 – Use AI Every Day Use AI as your learning partner, let it explain, test, review, and correct you. AI should guide you understand, not to replace your thinking. Network actively and ask mentors directly how they can help you access real opportunities, references, and experience.

Yes they do. You have problems on two fronts: plagiarism and AI. Of the two plagiarism is worst. In academic circles it's as bad as stealing. The way academics address this is by quoting (" ") and citing. And AI is the same as having someone else do your work. That too is unacceptable. If AI wrote it, you probably don't even know the source. So really that is the root cause. My suggestion is you use the AI sample as a big "hint" on your assignment. Read it carefully. Try to figure the main idea and key points. Then toss it away. Wait a few days and you write your own — no AI There are built in editors in MS Word and other online sites that will help you proof your writing. It will help correct spelling and grammer mistakes and suggest punctuation. But these are not perfect. So, you should read it very carefully. You should put it away for a day, at least, and read it out loud. If possible, have someone proof read it and make suggestions.

AI is one of many tools you might employ as you consider your future education. On the plus side, ChatGPT and other AI tools have ready access to a ton of data that can potentially inform you. (Note that Information Theory distinguishes between raw "data" and useful "information.") On the minus side, there is a lot of bad data out there, and both your AI tools and you need to sort it out. The old saying is, "Garbage In, Garbage Out" (aka GIGO), so you can be badly misinformed if you ask the wrong questions and/or ask a resource that depends on uncurated input. So, I'd say that it's okay to use whatever tools you can, including AI, as you proceed. Be aware that you need to ask the right questions of the right resources if you want to get valid answers.

I'm not sure A.I.s are capable of lying, as that implies understanding the difference between something true and something false, as opposed to understanding that something statistically is likely to appear next to a particular word in a particular sentence or whether it's statistically unlikely to appear next to a word in a particular sentence. When it comes to hallucinations though, boy can AI hallucinate! Frequent examples are when I have students write literary analysis papers. First, ChatGPT makes up all kinds of crazy page numbers and chapter numbers or names that don't exist. I've had students submit AI-generated papers in which the AI confidently asserts that the hero Grettir in Grettir's Saga was born with a birthmark in the shape of a dragon, indicating he is fated for heroic deeds, and quickly saves the village from a dragon attack. The AI claims this happened in Chapter Two, page 18 of the Jesse Byock translation of Grettir's Saga, as assigned to the class. In actually, Grettir isn't even born until Chapter Thirteen. Page 18 is still in the "notes about the translation" part of the book. In Chapter Two, Grettir's grandparents are first marrying and settling down. And Grettir doesn't have any birthmarks. And Grettir doesn't ever fight any dragons. And Grettir doesn't save any villages during his childhood, merely tortures a bunch of animals, fights with his dad, and pines for heroic deeds. So, in the space of an opening paragraph, the AI lies (i.e., hallucinates) in at least five ways. I'm not sure it's really "getting" the AI to lie though. The AI does it all on its own, without any additional prompting beyond asking it to write an essay about Grettir's childhood in the Jesse Byock translation of Grettir's Saga.

Agree and disagree. I have my students use NotebookLM in two ways. First, it is an efficient way to search the textbook for answers. I wrote my own textbook, so I can just create a notebook for each chapter. Second, a bit more complicated. Students "take a quiz" there but it's not for points. Rather, it is to generate new notes that challenge their understanding AFTER taking notes from reading that are a bit novel. Instead of just assigning notes and seeing what I get, I ask them to answer the same 5 questions for each section of the chapter: What's the big idea, why does it matter, what connections do you see, how has this changed your thinking, and what questions do you now have? At the end, they write a summary and their top 3 questions. These make it to a discussion forum where they then try answer each others questions. At semester's end, they write two papers: one to weave their summaries together and another that is a Top Ten FAQ for the course. Once they submit those notes (that I data mine for assessment and feedback), they get access to the NotebookLM to "take a quiz" that is not graded. Rather, for each question, regardless of whether they get it right or wrong, they must read the "Explain" response and identify how that changed their understanding, whether it addressed a question they had from earlier, and how confident they are at this point on this topic/subtopic. Regarding the data mining, I use AI to extract the unique questions (hundreds per chapter) and filter them semantically and thematically into a shorter list that I can deliver in person/writing and essentially answer everyone's questions efficiently. No way I could answer hundreds of questions per week per class.

Humans naturally categorize the world using hierarchical concepts (animal → dog → husky), while traditional neural networks mainly learn pixel patterns and fine details without understanding broader meanings. AliGNet from Google DeepMind aims to bridge this gap by aligning machine representations with human conceptual structures. The idea relies on representation learning, where neural networks convert images into embeddings (abstract feature vectors). When these features are properly disentangled (e.g., separating color, shape, lighting), the model can generalize better and understand objects by meaning rather than appearance. Interestingly, this aligns with ideas from cognitive science and even Plato’s theory of Forms, which argued that real knowledge comes from understanding abstract, stable concepts behind physical examples. Modern AI research is essentially trying to build systems that capture these invariant representations. #AI #DeepLearning #AliGNet #RepresentationLearning #MachineLearning #CognitiveScience #ArtificialIntelligence #ConceptLearning #Embeddings #Disentanglement #HumanAIAlignment #Plato #PhilosophyOfAI #AIResearch #FutureOfAI

The Felony in the Backpack: Why Your Child Should Never Unlock Their Phone at School (And, yes - It is Illegal for Schools to Rifle Through a Student's Cellphone) I was recently going through my Quora queue and stumbled on a very interesting question asking about parent and student rights regarding confiscated student property. Because of the intersection of innumerable legal issues surrounding property confiscation and criminal law, I felt it appropriate to provide a robust analysis. And, to top it all off, I can't find the original question any longer because I didn't save the draft properly—oops. But the topic is too important to let slide. After the various school violence tragedies we have all been forced to witness, we all understand that kids need to be safe at school. Fearing death throughout the day is not conducive to learning. But in American society, the rights of the people remain paramount. For decades, the Supreme Court has been forced to walk a tightrope between these competing interests, and the result is a legal landscape that every parent needs to understand before they send their child off to homeroom. The Supreme Court famously held in *Tinker v. Des Moines* that students do not "shed their constitutional rights... at the schoolhouse gate." There is no iron curtain separating the Constitution from the classroom, and parents should never feel ashamed to ensure their children know it. However, while the Constitution enters the building with the student, it changes form once inside. The courts have essentially decided that the school environment is a unique legal ecosystem where the rules of the street do not apply. This is most evident in how schools handle your child's property. In the outside world, the police generally need "Probable Cause" and a warrant to rifle through your belongings. Inside the school, under the precedent set by *New Jersey v. T.L.O.*, administrators need only "Reasonable Suspicion." This is a significantly lower bar. It means that if a teacher sees your child acting nervously near a locker, or receives a credible tip from another student, they often have enough legal cover to open that locker without your permission—and without a warrant. In this regard, the comparison to a correctional facility is not entirely hyperbole. Courts have generally held that students have a very low expectation of privacy regarding school lockers, viewing them as school property "loaned" to the student and subject to inspection at any time. The backpack is different. It is personal property and theoretically requires "individualized reasonable suspicion" to search. They cannot just search every bag in the classroom because a phone is missing; they need a reason to suspect your child specifically. However, we must be realistic about how this plays out in a hallway or a vice principal's office. Suspicion is rarely articulated clearly in the heat of the moment. Instead, it is often reconstructed months later, after lawyers have gotten involved. An administrator who searches a bag on a hunch and finds nothing simply sends the student back to class, and no one is the wiser. But if they find something, they will almost certainly recall—after the fact—that the student "looked nervous" or "smelled like weed," creating the legal cover they need to justify the intrusion retroactively. The danger here is that students are socialized to obey teachers, so they often give consent to a search when they don't have to. Students must understand that the moment a teacher's questions shift from normal school stuff—"Did you do your homework?" or "Where is your permission slip?"—to criminal investigation—"Who were you with at lunch?" or "Why do you like guns?"—that teacher is no longer an educator. In that moment, they are to be viewed as a police officer. Consenting to a backpack search in that scenario is no different than inviting a detective to rummage through your car. This concept extends to the most dangerous item in your child's possession: the "21st-century backpack," otherwise known as the smartphone. A cell phone is not just a container; it is a digital archive of the child's, and possibly even yours, entire life. While the Supreme Court (*Riley v. California*) has ruled that police generally need a warrant to search a phone, schools often ignore this boundary under the guise of "consent" (and frankly, teachers are rarely trained in constitutional rights anyway). They may confiscate a phone for a simple rule violation, like texting in class, and then use that as an excuse to browse through private photos and messages. This is where parents need to get real about what is actually on those phones. In today's world, teens "sext." They trade explicit pictures and test out adult conversations. Most parents view this as "experimenting" or a moral failing but don't necessarily see it as a crime. But the federal government views it as the production of child pornography, which carries a 15-year minimum prison sentence. Many parents assume their children are protected by free speech or "Romeo and Juliet" laws, but in *United States v. Streett*, Judge James O. Browning ruled that minors do not have a First Amendment right to sext and there is no Romeo and Juliet law for digital crimes. He held that federal laws prohibiting child pornography apply even to consensual texts between teenagers. This means if a school administrator bullies your child into unlocking their phone and finds sexting images, your child isn't just facing suspension. They could be facing a felony and lifetime registration as a sex offender. Nothing illustrates the danger of interacting with school officials once they have latched on to a criminal inquiry better than the tragic case of the Oxford High School shooting and the conviction of the shooter's parents, James and Jennifer Crumbley. Parents often ask if the Crumbleys "cooperated" with the school. The answer is yes—and that is exactly what helped convict them. On the morning of the shooting, they were called to the school because a teacher found a disturbing "sketch" on their son's math worksheet featuring a gun and a plea for help. The parents came to the school, met with counselors, and looked at the sketch. But because they viewed the meeting as a nuisance rather than a critical legal confrontation, they made fatal mistakes. They told the school they couldn't take their son home because they had to return to work, and they did not demand to search his backpack—which was sitting right there in the room, containing the handgun. The school administrators, covering their own liability, later testified against the parents, stating they were "caught off guard" by the parents' refusal to act. The school used the parents' presence and their specific statements in that meeting to build the narrative of "Gross Involuntary Manslaughter." Had the Crumbleys treated that meeting as an adversarial encounter—had they brought a lawyer or simply refused to speak until they understood the legal jeopardy—a lawyer would have likely demanded the bag be searched or the child be removed immediately. Instead, the parents walked into a trap where the school documented their negligence to save itself. I want to be clear before we go further: I am not implying that your kid is a criminal or engages in felonious behavior. In the world of criminal defense, we don't care what is actually on the phone or in the backpack. Our goal is to protect the right to keep it private. The old adage that "if you have nothing to hide, you have nothing to fear" is a dangerous fallacy. The government routinely twists innocent behavior to make it appear criminal. Consider the case of Richard Jewell, the security guard who saved lives at the 1996 Olympics. The FBI took his zealousness for his job and twisted it to fit a bomber profile. Innocent behavior became "evidence" of guilt. That is why privacy matters, regardless of innocence. To protect themselves from this trap, a child must first learn to recognize when a conversation has shifted from academic to investigatory. An academic question is safe: "Did you finish your essay?" But parents must teach their children to hear the alarm bells when the questions pivot to their behavior, their peers, or their property. Questions like "Whose bag is this?" or "Do you know anything about what happened in the bathroom?" are not educational inquiries; they are interrogations. Once that line is crossed, the burden of protection falls on the student. You must train your child to view school administrators in that moment not as "educators," but as government agents. The instruction to the child should be absolute: never speak the password, and never unlock the device. It does not matter if the request comes from a favorite teacher or the principal. The device is a vault, and the student holds the only key. If the school believes they have the legal authority to search the phone, they can attempt to bypass the encryption themselves or seek a warrant. By refusing to unlock it, the student is not being insubordinate; they are forcing the government to follow the law. School officials rely on the student's instinct to please adults. They will use the "Friendly Traitor" tactic, telling the student that if they just tell the truth, the school can help them. The reality is that once a student admits to a crime—whether it's drugs, weapons, or sexting—the administrator is often required by law to hand that info to the police. Parents must teach their children a simple script: "I will not answer any questions without my parents present." Finally, you must have a plan for when the school inevitably takes something. I recall a situation when I was in 8th grade where a friend was arrested and adjudicated delinquent because he accidentally brought a box cutter from his construction job to school. The teacher didn't see a hardworking kid; she saw a "weapon" and a Zero Tolerance violation. This is why immediate communication is vital. If anything is confiscated, your child should tell you immediately. If the item is perceptually dangerous or could have deeply personal secrets contained in it (like a cell phone), assume the worst. Do not try to talk to the school to "clear things up." Get a buffer between you and the government and call a lawyer. If the school has your child's phone, assume they are trying to break into it and presume that anything they find "can and will be used against" them. Ultimately, we must remember that public school teachers are not just educators; they are government officials bound by the Constitution just like the police. While the courts have granted them significant legal leeway to maintain order, that power is not absolute. Do yourself and your child a favor: teach them how to invoke their right to not open phones, and if anything is taken from them, demand its return. And if you really want to protect yourself, bring a lawyer if you are ever called to a meeting with school officials that could turn into an analysis of your child's "behavior." Protecting your child means understanding that in the eyes of the law, the principal's office can be just as dangerous as the precinct. Image Credit: Gemini - People and Places are fictitious

AI in academia offers many benefits, but there are notable disadvantages as well: 1. **Plagiarism and Academic Integrity**: AI can generate text that may be used to bypass academic work, raising concerns about plagiarism and the authenticity of student work. 2. **Dependence on Technology**: Excessive reliance on AI tools may reduce critical thinking and problem-solving skills among students and researchers. 3. **Bias and Fairness**: AI systems can perpetuate or amplify existing biases if they're trained on biased data, affecting the fairness of academic evaluations and research outcomes. 4. **Job Displacement**: Automation of certain academic tasks could lead to job displacement for administrative staff and even researchers if AI systems take over certain roles. 5. **Privacy Concerns**: AI systems often require large amounts of data, raising issues around the privacy and security of academic data and personal information. 6. **Quality Control**: AI-generated content might lack the nuanced understanding and critical analysis expected in academic work, potentially affecting the quality of research and publications.

When you are a student of machine learning, the first thing you do in front of a new problem is think of how you you could put it in a shape where it can be addressed using classical machine learning techniques, and then test a lot of different techniques with simple implementations to see how they behave and compare. And simple neural networks, such as perceptrons and multilayer perceptrons, are a rather classical machine learning technique. So you can bet they have been tried in a number of situations. If they are not readily used for some specific problem that is solved by one or more other ML methods, that is probably because that (or those) alternative method(s) perform better than simple NN for that problem. However, I am quite convinced that there are many problems that are currently not solved using modern machine learning methods in general (including NNs) because they are more difficult to express as machine learning problems than most, but that would significantly benefit from being viewed under that perspective.

This is a very good topic, and I completely agree that the current "Prussian" method of education is outdated. But my history teacher taught me decades ago that this is mandatory, so I'll say it, but it's much more important for you to know where to find specific information when you need it than to memorize a lot of information now that you'll probably forget anyway. However, some basic knowledge is still necessary, because otherwise people can easily be misled, see flat-earthers, so we cannot completely abandon the teaching of things that seem useless at present. The other is memorize learning, such as multiplication tables, which is necessary for children to learn in a certain way, to engrave the functioning of their brains in this way. Without this, they may not be able to remember even simple things. Another thing I like is the idea of AI tutors and tutoring. I see two dangers in this. One is that the current LLM hallucinates a lot and gives incorrect information, so it is unsuitable for teaching because it will only generate more flat-earthers. So only AI trained on supervised sources can be considered here. The other half of the issue is that if the child is not motivated, no matter what tool they are given, they will not use it. So what they talking about is a really-really good way, I like it! Anyway the current method needs to be transformed, but I don't think it should be discarded, but rather expanded, modified, and fine-tuned. You need basic knowledge, because if you only focus on making your child curious, you will end up with a lot of curious but easily misled children. You need basic knowledge to be able to filter out nonsense, and that can only be achieved if they also acquire the knowledge that "might be useful someday," but in a much smarter way and without unnecessary frills. For example, the balance of the second degree may not be important, or exactly when the Battle of "anything" took place. But I think it is critical that we know about the past and acquire basic physical and other knowledge. As is learning how to learn, how to search for information, how to question and filter out meaningful information from the noise. Especially in the current era of AI-generated content, when you can't even trust your own eyes and ears, it will be important that they can't be fooled, at least when it comes to basic things.

Yes — detecting cheating in online exams is possible, but not guaranteed. Effective detection combines technical controls, behavioral analysis, assessment design, and human review. Below is a structured framework covering methods, strengths, limitations and practical recommendations. What "cheating" means here Unauthorized collaboration (peers, tutors, exam services). Use of unauthorized resources (notes, websites, answer keys, textbooks). Identity fraud (impersonation, proxy test-takers). Automated assistance (AI, scripts, exam-taking tools). Exam environment manipulation (multiple device use, hidden materials). Detection techniques (technical and procedural) Identity and access controls Two-factor authentication, single sign-on, identity verification using government ID checks. Biometric checks: face recognition during login and/or throughout the session. Live or recorded proctoring to confirm the test-taker's identity. Proctoring modalities Live proctoring: human proctors monitor via webcam, intervene in real time. Automated proctoring: AI flags suspicious patterns (gaze deviation, multiple faces, background noise). Hybrid: AI for triage, human review for flagged events. Device and environment monitoring Lockdown browsers to prevent switching tabs, copying, printing, or running unauthorized apps. Screen recording and screen-sharing. Multi-camera setups or room scans at start to detect other people/materials. System integrity checks (detect virtual machines, developer tools, remote desktop). Behavioral and forensic analytics Keystroke and typing-pattern analysis (biometric typing signatures). Mouse-movement, answer-timing, and response-pattern analysis (detect improbable speed or pattern matching across students). Plagiarism detection for essays and code (text-matching, code-similarity tools). Statistical detection: clustering similar answer patterns, improbable answer distributions (e.g., identical wrong answers), or sudden performance spikes. Network and data signals IP address monitoring and geolocation anomalies. Simultaneous logins from multiple locations. Unusual data transfer patterns suggesting external help. Assessment design to reduce cheating Large question pools and randomized item selection. Versioning: different permutations or parameterized problems per candidate. Open-book appropriate tasks: focus on higher-order thinking, application, and synthesis rather than recall. Time limits tuned to reduce time for external consultation. Oral follow-ups, viva voce, or short synchronous defenses for high-stakes outputs. Strengths and limitations Strengths: Combination of methods raises cost and risk for cheaters, deters casual cheating. Automated tools scale and provide triage for human review. Assessment redesign reduces the value of simple answer sharing. Limitations: False positives: benign behaviors (e.g., looking away) can be flagged; heavy reliance on algorithms risks discrimination or bias. False negatives: sophisticated cheating (paid proctors, human proxies, remote collaborators) can evade detection. Privacy, legal, and accessibility concerns: continuous video/audio monitoring and biometrics may violate laws or institutional policies; accommodations must be maintained. Resource intensity: live proctoring and thorough human review are costly and time-consuming. Best-practice implementation (practical checklist) Define threat model and acceptable risk for the exam (low/medium/high stakes). Combine layered controls: identity verification + lockdown + proctoring + analytics. Use assessment design that reduces leakability: randomized items, open-ended questions, oral checks. Tune automated systems and establish human-review workflows for flagged events to reduce false positives. Ensure transparency: publish exam rules, allowed tools, and privacy policies; require consent where legally necessary. Provide accommodations workflow to avoid disadvantaging certain groups. Maintain an evidence-based adjudication process: collect logs, recordings, and analytic reports before taking disciplinary action. Regularly audit detection systems for fairness, accuracy, and compliance with laws (GDPR, FERPA, local regulations). When detection is infeasible or risky If privacy or legal constraints forbid intrusive monitoring, shift toward open-book, project-based assessments and frequent low-stakes checks that emphasize mastery over surveillance. Use oral exams, team projects with individual contribution proofs, or proctored in-person exams when high assurance is required. Conclusion Detecting cheating in online exams is feasible through layered technical, analytical and design measures, but no single tool is foolproof. The most reliable approach balances deterrence, robust detection with human review, assessment redesign to lower incentive/value of cheating, and attention to privacy, fairness, and legal constraints.

Machine Learning from First Principles ML is not magic. It is structured mathematics. 🧵 • Prediction = Function Approximation Find a function f(x) → y that works on unseen data. Not memorization. Generalization. • Data Is the Only Teacher No hand-written rules. Only examples + feedback from mistakes. • Learning = Optimization Minimize a number (loss). Lower loss → better predictions. That’s the central law of ML. • Loss Function Defines Success You must mathematically define what “good” means. If you can’t measure it, you can’t learn it. • Parameters Are the Knobs Models are functions with adjustable weights (θ). Learning = turning knobs to reduce error. • Gradient Descent = Adjustment Mechanism Compute slope → move opposite direction → repeat. Like rolling downhill on an error surface. • Generalization Is the Real Goal Zero training error is easy. Low error on unseen data is intelligence. • Bias–Variance Tradeoff Too simple → underfit. Too complex → overfit. Engineering lives in the balance. • Three Learning Modes – Supervised: learn from labeled examples – Unsupervised: discover hidden structure – Reinforcement: learn via reward signals • Overfitting Is Inevitable Without Constraints Large models can memorize noise. Regularization forces discipline. • Inductive Bias Makes Learning Possible You must assume something about the world. No assumptions → no generalization. • Deep Learning = Representation Learning Stop hand-crafting features. Let the system learn internal representations. Machine Learning is not hype. It is optimization + statistics + assumptions about reality. Execution matters. #MachineLearning #AI #FirstPrinciples #DeepLearning #DataScience #Engineering #STEM #Optimization

I was a Teacher's Assistant (TA) in university. I had to grade lab assignments (software). If there were 20 people in my lab, then I would get 20 very different assignments. No duplicates. It was trivially easy to catch people cheating. Why? Because your brain is not the same as any other brain even if you all work on exactly the same assignment. Note there were some guys who clearly copied each other but changed a few minor things (e.g. variable names). It was still very easy to tell they cheated. The structure/architecture was the same, the flow was the same in their assignments, even if there were text differences. Also, only about 10% of the assignments were "clean" enough to function/work correctly. The failure points were extremely unique. In other words you all will sound more similar when you get the right answer, but wrong answers are all over the map, they're chaotic. And yes, after a couple of assignments I could start to tell which assignment matched up with students. If one of them suddenly jumped how they approached an assignment, that was a red flag. That means, if you and a couple other people use ChatGPT, you're gonna get caught. If you are the only guy using ChatGPT there's a high likelihood you're gonna get caught.

I don't think anyone knows. But even before AI, surveys found that over 60% of college students cheat regularly. I've certainly seen this in the classes I teach. For years I've almost given up on fighting it, but one of my colleagues decided to really crack down on cheating one semester. He ended up catching and failing just over half his class. Rather than being commended, he got chewed out by the department chair. Cheating has become an accepted norm. If you are a student, there is actually social pressure to cheat. There is also grade pressure: you are competing against students who cheat. if you don't cheat, you're at a big disadvantage. My guess is that the percentage of students using AI to complete assignments is over 80%. As a professor, here is how I am responding: I give regular assignments, but they count almost nothing toward the grade. I explain to my students that, if they work together or use AI to complete the assignments, they won't learn the material. But it's their choice; I don't care if they cheat. I give frequent in-class exams. Except for the final, they are unannounced. During the exams, I use a technology called a "lockdown browser." Once the student accesses the exam, they cannot open any other window on their computer. That prevents them from messaging other students or using an AI. And of course, phones are banned during exams. So, the students who don't do their own work on the assignments do very poorly on the exams. But it's their choice, and I don't put any energy into worrying about whether or not they are cheating.

Teachers can use several methods to catch students cheating with AI: 1. Plagiarism detection software: Utilize tools like Turnitin or Copyscape to check for similarities between students' work and online sources. 2. Analyze writing style: Compare a student's previous work to their current assignment. Sudden changes in writing style could indicate cheating. 3. Monitor screen activity: Use software that allows remote monitoring of students' screens during online exams to detect unauthorized resources or external assistance. 4. Randomize questions: Create different versions of the same exam with randomized questions and answer choices to make cheating more challenging. 5. Webcam proctoring: Implement webcam monitoring during online exams to ensure students aren't receiving help from others or using unauthorized materials. 6. Check timestamps: Look for unusually fast completion times or prolonged breaks during online exams, which could suggest cheating. 7. In-person exams: For in-person assessments, use a combination of technology and vigilant proctoring to deter and detect cheating. 8. Promote academic integrity: educate students about the importance of academic honesty and the consequences of cheating to discourage such behavior. 9. Communication: Encourage students to report suspicious behavior by their peers, fostering a culture of academic integrity. Remember that while AI and technology can help in detecting cheating, they should be used in conjunction with other preventive measures and a focus on education about the importance of integrity in learning.

This would be an AI generator which does not have any filters on it. Most corpo AIs either image or text will have either built in filters or an API which ensures the AI's outputs are in line with the company policies. An uncensored AI is one which does not have these filters allowing it to attempt to output anything with the results varying. This last part is key because the AI will attempt to generate whatever is requested or instructed but if it wasn't in its training data it will not do very well. Most of the uncensored AIs are locally run, someone running the AI on their computer using its hardware to generate outputs. Because of this there is nothing between the user and the AI and it can thus be given any instructions.

"Predict" is a very vague word here. Do we mean predict trends? In a given city there will be X crimes of a certain type in a given year? Do we mean predict victims? Who is most likey to be a victim of crime? Do we mean specific crimes to be commited? This person will rob this bank between these dates? Do we mean predict crimes that have happened? This crime happened, we predict X was the prepetrator responsible? Do we mean to predict is a particular set of of observations constitutes a crime? Do we mean for an individual if they will commit any crime? Do we mean for an individual if they will be arrested for commiting a crime? There are a lot of different uses here - some more contoversial than others. Some I have direct experience of. Running through... Do we mean predict trends? In a given city there will be X crimes of a certain type in a given year? This is just forecasting. Using more data, more variables and more computationally intensive techniques doesn't fundamentally change anything. Its a cornerstone of public policy to predict resource needs. Its unexciting in many ways, but its a great use of ML/AI that can diliver some incremental benefits over previous approaches due to increased accuracy. It happens and has been happening for some time. Do we mean predict victims? Who is most likey to be a victim of crime? Are some people more at risk than others? If we need to allocate police resources to protect some communities (defined geographically, by age, by ethnicity or however we need) can we use AI to do it more efficiently? How do we use AI to minimise the risk to society overall? THis is another good use... but it has a small sliver of conroversy. Any time to alocate resources to one group at the expense of another there are losers as well as winners. An AI is then (even if tangentialy) causing some people to be more exposed to crime. In my eyes it is a valid payoff to protect the more vulnerable at the expense of the less vulnerable and most police services will be trying to prioritise resource allocation anyway and this doesn't change their objectives, but will just achieve them better. Do we mean specific crimes to be commited? This person will rob this bank between these dates? Othere than some very specific examples this is a bit far fetched. The specificity needed for both a person and a crime makes training a model a nightmare. Possibly with very common crimes - the person who shop lifts most days, or the person who gets into a fight every Saturday night, but then the value an AI brings to this problem is limited. We would be likely to have a very biased data set; we don't tend to have crime records for this but rather arrest records or conviction records. If not every criminal is as likey to have been arrested following a crime then this wil be screwy. If it were to work, then it would need the highest level of scrutiny for bias. Any bias in policing and in arrest patterns would be likely to propagate into the model. The kind of profiling to fall out would be problematic. There are also the issues around the actions to be taken - do you surveil the predicted perpetrators? The victims? How accurate do you need to be to warrent allocating a resource this way? A 1% success rate predicting a given crime at a given location on a given day is a remarkable feat - but is it enough to allocate resource by? Do we mean predict crimes that have happened? This crime happened, we predict X was the prepetrator responsible? This is AI supported investigation and used very cautiously, it can be effective. What this can be used for is to provide investigators with a generated list of similar crimes - similar descriptions, similar locations, patterns etc.. It isn't providing the answer, but expediting the investigation by helping to prioritise suspects and giving investigators more information and more relevant information. When it comes to an individual already subject to an investigation, it has little value. An AI is unikely to have a better perspective than a focussed investigative officer - the ability to pull through VAST amounts of data and to do this for a lot of crimes where resource limitations would otherwise be prohibitive. This needs a lot of scrutiny, depending on the decisions being made. Will it determine which crimes are investigated? Will it mean that certain groups of people become much more able to get away with crimes than others? Do we mean to predict if a particular set of of observations constitutes a crime? This is a common enough use case anyway - this is basically fraud detection for things like financial transactions. Getting more and more sophisticated is usually seen as a good thing. Do we mean for an individual if they will commit any crime?/Do we mean for an individual if they will be arrested for commiting a crime? This is close to the (in)famous COMPAS study predicting recidivism amongst offenders. It can be controversial, and like a lot of these things you need to be realy, really careful with training data. Moreover, you need to use it in the circumstances for which it was designed and must remember that rearest is not the same as reoffence. So broadly, I am in favour of using smart approaches in law enforcement but there are a lot of pitfalls here and a lot of safeguards needed. I have built some AI systems for police services and I know they take the ethics very seriously (in the UK at least) which is somewhat reasuring - even so there are still some occasional missteps.

Artificial Intelligence Exploitation Prevention Act (AIEPA) Section 1. Short Title This Act may be cited as the “Artificial Intelligence Exploitation Prevention Act.” Section 2. Purpose The purpose of this Act is to: Protect individuals — including celebrities and private citizens — from exploitation through artificial intelligence. Prevent the creation and distribution of non-consensual AI-generated likenesses, voices, and identities. Establish criminal and civil penalties for misuse of AI technology. Provide victims with legal remedies and enforcement support. Section 3. Definitions For purposes of this Act: Artificial Intelligence (AI): Any software or machine-based system capable of generating images, video, audio, text, or simulations that imitate a real person. Digital Likeness: A person’s face, body, voice, name, signature, or other identifying traits. Exploitative AI Content: AI-generated material that: Falsely portrays a person engaging in sexual acts. Depicts criminal or immoral behavior. Causes reputational harm. Is used for financial gain, harassment, fraud, blackmail, or defamation. Consent: Written, informed, and voluntary agreement from the individual depicted. Section 4. Criminal Offenses (A) Non-Consensual AI Exploitation Any person who knowingly creates, distributes, sells, or publishes exploitative AI content of another individual without consent shall be guilty of a felony. Penalties: Fine up to $250,000 Imprisonment up to 5 years Or both (B) Aggravated Offense If the violation involves: A minor Financial extortion or blackmail Repeated offenses Intent to cause severe emotional distress Penalties: Fine up to $500,000 Imprisonment up to 10 years Mandatory restitution to the victim Section 5. Civil Remedies Victims may: Sue for statutory damages of not less than $100,000 per violation. Seek court-ordered removal of the AI content. Recover attorney fees and punitive damages. Section 6. Platform Responsibility Digital platforms that knowingly host exploitative AI content and fail to remove it within 48 hours of verified notice may be subject to civil penalties. Section 7. Jurisdiction This law applies to: All U.S. citizens. Any AI content accessible within the United States. Foreign actors targeting U.S. residents. Section 8. Enforcement The Department of Justice shall establish a specialized AI Exploitation Task Force to investigate and prosecute violations.

2022–2023 UPDATE I feel like many people think of AI as some superhuman robotic mind. However, it is basically just a set of algorithms. Yet it is very helpful for the cybersecurity field. If you don't want to read a lot, long story short: With the help of AI in cybersecurity, you can promptly analyze big volumes of data based on the available information and detect threats. Note: AI doesn't detect only already known threats. It can 'connect the dots' between different characteristics. Additionally, AI can go through the incidents to find the patterns that can be suspicious, indicating vulnerabilities or exploits. Also, it helps to gather threat intel information. Now, if you want to dive deeper into the subject. What are the main advantages of AI? It can be used perfectly to: Work with a lot of data Speed up the work processes Scan the environment Connect various characteristics to localize potential threats Automatically respond to threats Learn while working However, it isn't a holy grail because there are several downsides, such as: You will still get false positives and false negatives. AI can only do an analysis based on the data it has. With that said, you can't entirely give the threat hunting in the hands of AI because the essence of threat hunting is looking for something that isn't yet discovered. There are various concerns about the possibility of hackers aiming AI to make a program think that the processes happening in the system are normal, even though they aren't. Based on what I've mentioned before, you've probably guessed the use cases in cybersecurity. I would highlight the following: Threat detection and network scanning. Diagnosis and response. Automated report on cyber threat intelligence(CTI).

I think it's the only way for the educational system to provide a meaningful assessment of students' work. This is not a new problem, however. Studies have found that about 60% of university students cheat regularly. And it is very unfair to students who don't cheat. It also gives a very false picture of a student's skills to prospective employers. Students who cheat - whether they are using AI or other humans to do their work - are not learning the material, and yet they can get high grades in the traditional system. I think the solution is pretty straightforward, and it's what I am now doing in the classes I teach: (1). Give assignments as usual. Review them and provide feedback, but don't count them toward the grade. Students can do the assignments and learn from them; they can cheat and have and AI or another person do the work; or they can skip the assignments entirely. It's up to them. (2). Have regular in-class exams. The grade is based on these exams. To prevent cheating, we use something called a "lockdown browser." Once the student starts the exam, they cannot access any other window on their computer. They can't look things up, they can't access AI, they can't message their friends. And of course, phones are prohibited during the exam. If I see a phone, the student gets a 0 on the exam.

Here's an essay about the 💀 emoji: --- The 💀 Emoji: A Symbol of Humor, Death, and Digital Expression In the ever-evolving landscape of digital communication, emojis have become an essential part of how we express emotions, thoughts, and reactions in a more succinct, visual manner. Among the vast array of emojis, the 💀 emoji stands out as a particularly intriguing and multifaceted symbol. While it depicts a human skull, often associated with death and danger, its meaning in digital conversations has evolved into something much more playful and humorous. This essay will explore the different contexts in which the 💀 emoji is used, its cultural significance, and how it has found a place in the modern lexicon of internet communication. A Representation of Death and the Dark Historically, the skull has been a symbol tied to death, danger, and mortality. It has appeared in art, literature, and popular culture to represent the end of life, decay, and the inevitability of death. The 💀 emoji, in its most literal sense, carries these associations, reminding users of the transient nature of life. For example, in discussions about horror films, gothic themes, or even the inevitability of death itself, this emoji might be used to add an element of seriousness or to signal a dark topic. However, in the context of emoji use, the 💀 symbol has taken on an entirely different life. The Humor of "Dead" Laughter One of the most popular uses of the 💀 emoji today is as a reaction to something extremely funny. The phrase “I'm dead” has been a part of internet slang for years, usually indicating that something is so funny it has metaphorically killed the person with laughter. The 💀 emoji perfectly complements this expression, as it portrays the idea of "dying" from amusement. When someone shares a particularly hilarious joke or meme, users might respond with a 💀 emoji to signify that they are “dead” from laughter, amplifying the comedic effect. This usage of the 💀 emoji emphasizes how digital communication is heavily influenced by humor and exaggeration. It’s not used to signal actual death but to reflect a state of being overwhelmed by something funny. In this way, it is an expression of lightheartedness and is often used among friends or in more informal contexts. The Evolution of Digital Language The use of emojis like 💀 also illustrates how language evolves in the digital era. Text communication can sometimes lack the tone and context that face-to-face interactions provide, leading to misunderstandings. Emojis bridge this gap, adding emotional nuance to otherwise flat text. The 💀 emoji, in particular, is a prime example of how a symbol that may have once been somber has been recontextualized in the playful, fast-paced world of internet culture. Additionally, the adoption of emojis across platforms (social media, texting, gaming, etc.) has created a form of shorthand in communication. The 💀 emoji, like many others, is understood instantly and transcends language barriers. It speaks to the power of visual cues in modern communication, offering a universal way to express complex emotions with a single character. Cultural Significance and Memes In popular culture, memes are central to the internet's identity, and the 💀 emoji has found a particular niche within them. It has been incorporated into countless memes, often in exaggerated or absurd contexts. Its use in meme culture reflects a broader trend where images and symbols take on new meanings, often detached from their original cultural significance. The playful nature of memes means that even symbols related to dark themes, like death, can be used in a carefree and light-hearted manner. The 💀 emoji, then, has become a symbol not of morbidity, but of the absurdity and humor that characterize the internet's reaction to the serious and the everyday. Conclusion In conclusion, the 💀 emoji is a prime example of how symbols and imagery evolve in the context of modern digital communication. Initially associated with death, it has transformed into a versatile symbol that reflects humor, exaggeration, and the shared language of the internet. Whether used to convey dark themes, signify laughter, or simply add emphasis to a statement, the 💀 emoji has become an integral part of how we express ourselves online. It highlights the dynamic nature of language in the digital age, where even the darkest symbols can find new life in the most unexpected ways.
